infobox.value: ([/kəˈnɛtɪkət/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Connecticut.ogg "File:En-us-Connecticut.ogg") [*kə-NET-ih-kət*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[10]](#cite_note-11) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") region of the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). It borders [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") to the east, [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") to the north, [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") to the west, and [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") to the south. Its capital is [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), and its most populous city is [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"). Connecticut lies between the major hubs of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") along the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), where the [New York-Newark Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), which includes four of Connecticut's seven largest cities, extends into the southwestern part of the state.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Connecticut is the [third-smallest state by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") after Rhode Island and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and the [29th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") with more than 3.6 million residents as of 2024,[[12]](#cite_note-ConnecticutQuickFacts-13) ranking it fourth among the [most densely populated U.S. states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density").

**Connecticut** ([/kəˈnɛtɪkət/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Connecticut.ogg "File:En-us-Connecticut.ogg") [*kə-NET-ih-kət*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[10]](#cite_note-11) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") region of the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). It borders [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") to the east, [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") to the north, [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") to the west, and [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") to the south. Its capital is [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), and its most populous city is [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"). Connecticut lies between the major hubs of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") along the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), where the [New York-Newark Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), which includes four of Connecticut's seven largest cities, extends into the southwestern part of the state.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Connecticut is the [third-smallest state by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") after Rhode Island and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and the [29th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") with more than 3.6 million residents as of 2024,[[12]](#cite_note-ConnecticutQuickFacts-13) ranking it fourth among the [most densely populated U.S. states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density").

The state is named after the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River"), the longest in New England, which roughly bisects the state and drains into the Long Island Sound between the towns of [Old Saybrook](/wiki/Old_Saybrook,_Connecticut "Old Saybrook, Connecticut") and [Old Lyme](/wiki/Old_Lyme,_Connecticut "Old Lyme, Connecticut"). The name of the river is in turn derived from anglicized spellings of *Quinnetuket*, a [Mohegan-Pequot](/wiki/Mohegan-Pequot_language "Mohegan-Pequot language") word for "long tidal river".[[13]](#cite_note-14) Before the arrival of the first European settlers, the region was inhabited by various [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") tribes. In 1633, the [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") established a small, short-lived settlement called [House of Hope](/wiki/House_of_Hope_(fort) "House of Hope (fort)") in Hartford. Half of Connecticut was initially claimed by the Dutch colony [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), which included much of the land between the Connecticut and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") Rivers, although the first major settlements were established by the English around the same time. [Thomas Hooker](/wiki/Thomas_Hooker "Thomas Hooker") led a band of followers from the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") to form the [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony"), while other settlers from Massachusetts founded the [Saybrook Colony](/wiki/Saybrook_Colony "Saybrook Colony") and the [New Haven Colony](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony"); both had merged into the first by 1664.

Connecticut's official nickname, the "Constitution State", refers to the [Fundamental Orders](/wiki/Fundamental_Orders_of_Connecticut "Fundamental Orders of Connecticut") adopted by the Connecticut Colony in 1639, which is considered by some to be the first written constitution in Western history.[[14]](#cite_note-15) As one of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") that rejected British rule during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), Connecticut was influential in the development of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). In 1787, [Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman") and [Oliver Ellsworth](/wiki/Oliver_Ellsworth "Oliver Ellsworth"), state delegates to the [Constitutional Convention](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)"), proposed [a compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise") between the [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia_Plan "Virginia Plan") and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey_Plan "New Jersey Plan") Plans;[[15]](#cite_note-16) its [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") structure for [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), with a respectively proportional and equal representation of the states in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), was adopted and remains to this day. In January 1788, Connecticut became the fifth state to ratify the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States").

Connecticut is a developed and affluent state, performing well on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_Human_Development_Index_score "List of U.S. states and territories by Human Development Index score") and on [different metrics of income](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_income "List of U.S. states and territories by income") except for [equality](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_income_inequality "List of U.S. states and territories by income inequality"). It is home to a number of prestigious educational institutions, including [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") in [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"), as well as other [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_colleges_in_the_United_States "Liberal arts colleges in the United States") and private [boarding schools](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school") in and around the "[Knowledge Corridor](/wiki/Hartford%E2%80%93Springfield "Hartford–Springfield")". Due to its geography, Connecticut has maintained a strong maritime tradition; the [United States Coast Guard Academy](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Academy "United States Coast Guard Academy") is located in [New London](/wiki/New_London,_Connecticut "New London, Connecticut") by the [Thames River](/wiki/Thames_River_(Connecticut) "Thames River (Connecticut)"). The state is also associated with the aerospace industry through major companies [Pratt & Whitney](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney "Pratt & Whitney") and [Sikorsky Aircraft](/wiki/Sikorsky_Aircraft "Sikorsky Aircraft") headquartered in [East Hartford](/wiki/East_Hartford,_Connecticut "East Hartford, Connecticut") and [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_Connecticut "Stratford, Connecticut"), respectively. Historically a manufacturing center for arms, hardware, and timepieces,[[16]](#cite_note-17) Connecticut, as with the rest of the region, had transitioned into an economy based on the financial, insurance, and real estate sectors; many multinational firms providing such services can be found concentrated in the state capital of [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") and along the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(Connecticut) "Gold Coast (Connecticut)") in [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Connecticut](/wiki/History_of_Connecticut "History of Connecticut")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Ctcolony.png/500px-Ctcolony.png)](/wiki/File:Ctcolony.png)

A map of the Connecticut, New Haven, and Saybrook colonies

### First people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First people")]

The name Connecticut is derived from the [Mohegan-Pequot](/wiki/Mohegan-Pequot_language "Mohegan-Pequot language") word that has been translated as "long tidal river" and "upon the long river",[[17]](#cite_note-Project-18) both referring to the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River"). Evidence of human presence in the Connecticut region dates to as far back as 10,000 years ago. Stone tools were used for hunting, fishing, and woodworking.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Semi-nomadic in lifestyle, these peoples moved seasonally to take advantage of various resources in the area.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) They shared languages based on [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages").[[22]](#cite_note-23) The Connecticut region was inhabited by many [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes that can be grouped into the [Nipmuc](/wiki/Nipmuc "Nipmuc"), the Sequin or "River Indians" (which included the [Tunxis](/wiki/Tunxis "Tunxis"), [Schaghticoke](/wiki/Schaghticoke_people "Schaghticoke people"), [Podunk](/wiki/Podunk_people "Podunk people"), [Wangunk](/wiki/Wangunk "Wangunk"), [Hammonasset](/wiki/Hammonasset_people "Hammonasset people"), and [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac "Quinnipiac")), the [Mattabesec](/wiki/Mattabesec "Mattabesec") or "Wappinger Confederacy" and the [Pequot-Mohegan](/wiki/Pequot "Pequot").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Some of these groups still reside in Connecticut, including the [Mohegans](/wiki/Mohegan_people "Mohegan people"), the [Pequots](/wiki/Pequot_people "Pequot people"), and the [Paugusetts](/wiki/Golden_Hill_Paugussett_Indian_Nation "Golden Hill Paugussett Indian Nation").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### Colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial period")]

Main articles: [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), [Saybrook Colony](/wiki/Saybrook_Colony "Saybrook Colony"), [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony"), [New Haven Colony](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony"), [New England Confederation](/wiki/New_England_Confederation "New England Confederation"), and [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England")

Dutchman [Adriaen Block](/wiki/Adriaen_Block "Adriaen Block") was the first European explorer in Connecticut.[[25]](#cite_note-26) He explored the region in 1614. [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") fur traders then sailed up the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River"), calling it Versche Rivier ("Fresh River") and building a fort at Dutch Point in Hartford, which they named "House of Hope" ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Huis van Hoop*).[[26]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/3c_Connecticut_Tercentenary%2CThe_Charter_Oak_single%2C_1935_issue.jpg/330px-3c_Connecticut_Tercentenary%2CThe_Charter_Oak_single%2C_1935_issue.jpg)](/wiki/File:3c_Connecticut_Tercentenary,The_Charter_Oak_single,_1935_issue.jpg)

On April 26, 1935, the U.S. Post Office issued a postage stamp commemorating the 300th anniversary of the settlement of the Connecticut colony.

The Connecticut Colony originally consisted of several smaller settlements in Windsor, Wethersfield, Saybrook, Hartford, and New Haven. The first English settlers came in 1633 and settled at Windsor, then at Wethersfield the following year.[[27]](#cite_note-28) [John Winthrop the Younger](/wiki/John_Winthrop_the_Younger "John Winthrop the Younger") of Massachusetts received a commission to create [Saybrook Colony](/wiki/Saybrook_Colony "Saybrook Colony") at the mouth of the Connecticut River in 1635.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

A large group of [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") arrived in 1636 from [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony"), led by [Thomas Hooker](/wiki/Thomas_Hooker "Thomas Hooker"), who established the [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony") at Hartford.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The [Fundamental Orders of Connecticut](/wiki/Fundamental_Orders_of_Connecticut "Fundamental Orders of Connecticut") were adopted in January 1639, and have been described as the first constitutional document in America.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The Quinnipiack Colony[[31]](#cite_note-32) was established by [John Davenport](/wiki/John_Davenport_(clergyman) "John Davenport (clergyman)"), [Theophilus Eaton](/wiki/Theophilus_Eaton "Theophilus Eaton"), and others at New Haven in March 1638. The New Haven Colony had its own constitution called "The Fundamental Agreement of the New Haven Colony", signed on June 4, 1639.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Each settlement was an independent political entity, established without official sanction of the English Crown.[[33]](#cite_note-34) In 1662, Winthrop traveled to England and obtained a charter from [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") which united the settlements of Connecticut.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Historically significant colonial settlements included [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Connecticut "Windsor, Connecticut") (1633), [Wethersfield](/wiki/Wethersfield,_Connecticut "Wethersfield, Connecticut") (1634), [Saybrook](/wiki/Deep_River,_Connecticut "Deep River, Connecticut") (1635), [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") (1636), [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") (1638), [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_Connecticut "Fairfield, Connecticut") (1639), [Guilford](/wiki/Guilford,_Connecticut "Guilford, Connecticut") (1639), [Milford](/wiki/Milford,_Connecticut "Milford, Connecticut") (1639), [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_Connecticut "Stratford, Connecticut") (1639), [Farmington](/wiki/Farmington,_Connecticut "Farmington, Connecticut") (1640), [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut") (1641), and [New London](/wiki/New_London,_Connecticut "New London, Connecticut") (1646).

The [Pequot War](/wiki/Pequot_War "Pequot War") marked the first significant clash between colonists and Native Americans in New England. The Pequot had been aggressively extending their area of control at the expense of the Wampanoag to the north, Narragansett (east), Connecticut River Valley Algonquian tribes and the Mohegan (west), and Lenape Algonquian people (south).[[35]](#cite_note-auto2-36) Meanwhile, the Pequot had been reacting with increasing aggression to colonial territorial expansion. In response to the 1636 murder of an English privateer and his crew, followed by the murder of a trader,[[35]](#cite_note-auto2-36) colonists raided a Pequot village on [Block Island](/wiki/Block_Island "Block Island"). The Pequots laid siege to Saybrook Colony's garrison that autumn, then raided Wethersfield in the spring of 1637. Organizing a band of militia and allies from the Mohegan and Narragansett tribes, colonists declared war and attacked a Pequot village on the [Mystic River](/wiki/Mystic_River_(Connecticut) "Mystic River (Connecticut)"). Death toll estimates range between 300 and 700 Pequots. After suffering another major loss at a battle in [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_Connecticut "Fairfield, Connecticut"), the Pequots sued for peace.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Connecticut's original Charter in 1662 granted it all the land to the "South Sea"—that is, to the Pacific Ocean.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The [Hartford Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Hartford_(1650) "Treaty of Hartford (1650)") with the Dutch was signed on September 19, 1650, but never ratified by the British, stated the western boundary of Connecticut ran north from [Greenwich Bay](/wiki/Greenwich,_Connecticut "Greenwich, Connecticut") for a distance of 20 miles (32 km),[[38]](#cite_note-BOWEN-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FLICK-40) "provided the said line come not within 10 miles [16 km] of Hudson River".[[38]](#cite_note-BOWEN-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FLICK-40) This agreement was observed by both sides until war erupted between England and The Netherlands in 1652. Conflict continued concerning colonial limits until the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") captured [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") in 1664.[[38]](#cite_note-BOWEN-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FLICK-40)

Most Colonial royal grants were for long east–west strips. Connecticut took its grant seriously and established a ninth county between the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") and [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") named [Westmoreland County](/wiki/Westmoreland_County,_Connecticut "Westmoreland County, Connecticut"). This resulted in the brief [Pennamite-Yankee Wars](/wiki/Pennamite-Yankee_Wars "Pennamite-Yankee Wars") with [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Yale College was established in 1701, providing Connecticut with an important institution to educate clergy and civil leaders.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The Congregational church dominated religious life in the colony and, by extension, town affairs in many parts.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

With more than 600 miles (970 km) of coastline including along its navigable rivers,[[43]](#cite_note-44) Connecticut developed the antecedents of a maritime tradition during its colonial years that would later produce booms in shipbuilding, marine transport, naval support, seafood production, and leisure boating.

Historical records list the *Tryall* as the first vessel built in Connecticut Colony, in 1649 at a site on the Connecticut River in modern Wethersfield.[[44]](#cite_note-45) In the two decades leading up to 1776 and the American Revolution, Connecticut boatyards launched about 100 [sloops](/wiki/Sloop "Sloop"), [schooners](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner") and [brigs](/wiki/Brig "Brig") according to a database of U.S. customs records maintained online by the [Mystic Seaport Museum](/wiki/Mystic_Seaport "Mystic Seaport"), the largest being the 180-ton *Patient Mary* launched in New Haven in 1763.[[45]](#cite_note-auto-46) Connecticut's first [lighthouse](/wiki/Lighthouse "Lighthouse") was constructed in 1760 at the mouth of the [Thames River](/wiki/Thames_River_(Connecticut) "Thames River (Connecticut)") with the [New London Harbor Lighthouse](/wiki/New_London_Harbor_Light "New London Harbor Light").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

#### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

Main article: [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/LowsCTmap.jpeg/330px-LowsCTmap.jpeg)](/wiki/File:LowsCTmap.jpeg)

A 1799 map of Connecticut which shows [The Oblong](/wiki/The_Oblong "The Oblong"), from *[Low's Encyclopaedia](/wiki/Low%27s_Encyclopaedia "Low's Encyclopaedia")*

Connecticut designated four delegates to the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") who signed the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"): [Samuel Huntington](/wiki/Samuel_Huntington_(statesman) "Samuel Huntington (statesman)"), [Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman"), [William Williams](/wiki/William_Williams_(Continental_Congress) "William Williams (Continental Congress)"), and [Oliver Wolcott](/wiki/Oliver_Wolcott "Oliver Wolcott").[[47]](#cite_note-48) Connecticut's legislature authorized the outfitting of six new regiments in 1775, in the wake of the clashes between British regulars and Massachusetts militia at Lexington and Concord. There were some 1,200 Connecticut troops on hand at the [Battle of Bunker Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_Bunker_Hill "Battle of Bunker Hill") in June 1775.[[48]](#cite_note-49) In 1775, [David Bushnell](/wiki/David_Bushnell_(inventor) "David Bushnell (inventor)") invented the [*Turtle*](/wiki/Turtle_(submersible) "Turtle (submersible)") which the following year launched the first submarine attack in history, unsuccessfully against a British warship at anchor in New York Harbor.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

In 1777, the British got word of [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") supplies in [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury,_Connecticut "Danbury, Connecticut"), and they landed an expeditionary force of some 2,000 troops in [Westport](/wiki/Westport,_Connecticut "Westport, Connecticut"). This force marched to Danbury, then destroyed homes and much of the depot. Continental Army troops and militia led by General [David Wooster](/wiki/David_Wooster "David Wooster") and General [Benedict Arnold](/wiki/Benedict_Arnold "Benedict Arnold") engaged them on their return march at [Ridgefield](/wiki/Battle_of_Ridgefield "Battle of Ridgefield") in 1777.[[50]](#cite_note-51) For the winter of 1778–79, General [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") decided to split the Continental Army into three divisions encircling [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), where British General Sir [Henry Clinton](/wiki/Henry_Clinton_(American_War_of_Independence) "Henry Clinton (American War of Independence)") had taken up winter quarters.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Major General [Israel Putnam](/wiki/Israel_Putnam "Israel Putnam") chose Redding as the winter encampment quarters for some 3,000 regulars and militia under his command. The Redding encampment allowed Putnam's soldiers to guard the replenished supply depot in [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury,_Connecticut "Danbury, Connecticut") and to support any operations along Long Island Sound and the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") Valley.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Some of the men were veterans of the winter encampment at [Valley Forge](/wiki/Valley_Forge "Valley Forge"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), the previous winter. Soldiers at the Redding camp endured supply shortages, cold temperatures, and significant snow, with some historians dubbing the encampment "Connecticut's Valley Forge".[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The state was also the launching site for a number of raids against Long Island orchestrated by [Samuel Holden Parsons](/wiki/Samuel_Holden_Parsons "Samuel Holden Parsons") and [Benjamin Tallmadge](/wiki/Benjamin_Tallmadge "Benjamin Tallmadge"),[[54]](#cite_note-55) and provided soldiers and material for the war effort, especially to Washington's army outside New York City. General [William Tryon](/wiki/William_Tryon "William Tryon") [raided the Connecticut coast](/wiki/Tryon%27s_raid "Tryon's raid") in July 1779, focusing on New Haven, Norwalk, and Fairfield.[[55]](#cite_note-56) New London and Groton Heights [were raided](/wiki/Battle_of_Groton_Heights "Battle of Groton Heights") in September 1781 by Benedict Arnold, who had turned traitor to the British.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

At the outset of the American Revolution, the Continental Congress assigned [Nathaniel Shaw Jr.](/wiki/Nathaniel_Shaw "Nathaniel Shaw") of New London as its naval agent in charge of recruiting [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") to seize British vessels as opportunities presented, with nearly 50 operating out of the Thames River which eventually drew the reprisal from the British force led by Arnold.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

### Early statehood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early statehood")]

#### Early national period and industrial revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early national period and industrial revolution")]

Connecticut [ratified](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Ratification_of_the_Constitution "History of the United States Constitution") the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") on January 9, 1788, becoming the fifth state.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1)

The state prospered during the era following the American Revolution, as mills and textile factories were built and seaports flourished from trade[[58]](#cite_note-59) and fisheries. After Congress established in 1790 the predecessor to the [U.S. Revenue Cutter Service](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service") that would evolve into the U.S. Coast Guard, President Washington assigned Jonathan Maltbie as one of seven masters to enforce customs regulations, with Maltbie monitoring the southern New England coast with a 48-foot [cutter](/wiki/Cutter_(boat) "Cutter (boat)") sloop named [*Argus*](/wiki/USRC_Argus "USRC Argus").[[59]](#cite_note-60)

In 1786, Connecticut ceded territory to the U.S. government that became part of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"). The state retained land extending across the northern part of modern Ohio called the [Connecticut Western Reserve](/wiki/Connecticut_Western_Reserve "Connecticut Western Reserve").[[60]](#cite_note-United_States_Summary_2010,_page_V-5-61) The [Western Reserve](/wiki/Western_Reserve "Western Reserve") section was settled largely by people from Connecticut, and they brought Connecticut place names to Ohio.

Connecticut made agreements with Pennsylvania and New York which extinguished the land claims within those states' boundaries and created the [Connecticut Panhandle](/wiki/Connecticut_Panhandle "Connecticut Panhandle"). The state then ceded the Western Reserve in 1800 to the federal government,[[60]](#cite_note-United_States_Summary_2010,_page_V-5-61) which brought it to its existing boundaries (other than minor adjustments with Massachusetts).

### Nineteenth century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Nineteenth century")]

For the first time in 1800, Connecticut shipwrights launched more than 100 vessels in a single year. Over the following decade up to the doorstep of renewed hostilities with Britain that sparked the War of 1812, Connecticut boatyards constructed close to 1,000 vessels, the most productive stretch of any decade in the nineteenth century.[[45]](#cite_note-auto-46)

During the war, the British launched raids in [Stonington](/wiki/Stonington,_Connecticut "Stonington, Connecticut") and [Essex](/wiki/Essex,_Connecticut "Essex, Connecticut") and [blockaded](/wiki/Blockade "Blockade") vessels in the Thames River. [Derby](/wiki/Derby,_Connecticut "Derby, Connecticut") native [Isaac Hull](/wiki/Isaac_Hull "Isaac Hull") became Connecticut's best-known naval figure to win renown during the conflict, as captain of the [USS *Constitution*](/wiki/USS_Constitution "USS Constitution").

The British blockade during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") hurt exports and bolstered the influence of Federalists who opposed the war.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The cessation of imports from Britain stimulated the construction of factories to manufacture textiles and machinery. Connecticut came to be recognized as a major center for manufacturing, due in part to the inventions of [Eli Whitney](/wiki/Eli_Whitney "Eli Whitney") and other early innovators of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").[[62]](#cite_note-63)

The war led to the development of fast [clippers](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper") that helped extend the reach of New England merchants to the Pacific and Indian oceans. The first half of the nineteenth century saw as well a rapid rise in whaling, with New London emerging as one of the New England industry's three biggest home ports after [Nantucket](/wiki/Nantucket,_Massachusetts "Nantucket, Massachusetts") and [New Bedford](/wiki/New_Bedford,_Massachusetts "New Bedford, Massachusetts").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The state was known for its political conservatism, typified by its Federalist party and the Yale College of [Timothy Dwight](/wiki/Timothy_Dwight_IV "Timothy Dwight IV"). The foremost intellectuals were Dwight and [Noah Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster"),[[64]](#cite_note-65) who compiled his great dictionary in New Haven. Religious tensions polarized the state, as the Congregational Church struggled to maintain traditional viewpoints, in alliance with the Federalists. The failure of the [Hartford Convention](/wiki/Hartford_Convention "Hartford Convention") in 1814 hurt the Federalist cause, with the [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") gaining control in 1817.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Connecticut had been governed under the "[Fundamental Orders](/wiki/Fundamental_Orders_of_Connecticut "Fundamental Orders of Connecticut")" since 1639, but the state adopted a new constitution in 1818.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

#### Civil war era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Civil war era")]

Main article: [Connecticut in the American Civil War](/wiki/Connecticut_in_the_American_Civil_War "Connecticut in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/View_of_New_London%2C_Connecticut%2C_from_the_Shore_Road.jpg/250px-View_of_New_London%2C_Connecticut%2C_from_the_Shore_Road.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_New_London,_Connecticut,_from_the_Shore_Road.jpg)

View of New London in 1854

Connecticut manufacturers played a major role in supplying the Union forces with weapons and supplies during the [Civil War](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)"). The state furnished 55,000 men, formed into thirty full regiments of infantry, including two in the [U.S. Colored Troops](/wiki/U.S._Colored_Troops "U.S. Colored Troops"), with several Connecticut men becoming generals. The Navy attracted 250 officers and 2,100 men, and Glastonbury native [Gideon Welles](/wiki/Gideon_Welles "Gideon Welles") was Secretary of the Navy. [James H. Ward](/wiki/James_H._Ward "James H. Ward") of Hartford was the first U.S. Naval Officer killed in the Civil War.[[67]](#cite_note-68) Connecticut casualties included 2,088 killed in combat, 2,801 dying from disease, and 689 dying in Confederate prison camps.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)

A surge of national unity in 1861 brought thousands flocking to the colors from every town and city. However, as the war became a crusade to end slavery, many Democrats (especially Irish Catholics) pulled back. The Democrats took a pro-slavery position and included many [Copperheads](/wiki/Copperhead_(politics) "Copperhead (politics)") willing to let the South secede. The intensely fought 1863 election for governor was narrowly won by the Republicans.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

#### Second industrial revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Second industrial revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Connecticut1895.jpg/500px-Connecticut1895.jpg)](/wiki/File:Connecticut1895.jpg)

1895 map from [Rand McNally](/wiki/Rand_McNally "Rand McNally")

Connecticut's extensive industry, dense population, flat terrain, and wealth encouraged the construction of railroads starting in 1839. By 1840, 102 miles (164 km) of line were in operation, growing to 402 miles (647 km) in 1850 and 601 miles (967 km) in 1860.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

The [New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad](/wiki/New_York,_New_Haven_and_Hartford_Railroad "New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad"), called the *New Haven* or "The Consolidated", became the dominant Connecticut railroad company after 1872. [J. P. Morgan](/wiki/J._P._Morgan "J. P. Morgan") began financing the major New England railroads in the 1890s, dividing territory so that they would not compete. The New Haven purchased 50 smaller companies, including steamship lines, and built a network of light rails (electrified trolleys) that provided inter-urban transportation for all of southern New England. By 1912, the New Haven operated over 2,000 miles (3,200 km) of track with 120,000 employees.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

As steam-powered passenger ships proliferated after the Civil War, [Noank](/wiki/Noank,_Connecticut "Noank, Connecticut") produced the two largest built in Connecticut during the nineteenth century, with the 332-foot wooden steam [paddle wheeler](/wiki/Steam_paddler "Steam paddler") *Rhode Island* launched in 1882, and the 345-foot paddle wheeler *Connecticut* seven years later. Connecticut shipyards launched more than 165 steam-powered vessels in the nineteenth century.[[45]](#cite_note-auto-46)

In 1875, the first telephone exchange in the world was established in New Haven.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### Twentieth century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Twentieth century")]

#### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: World War I")]

When World War I broke out in 1914, Connecticut became a major supplier of weaponry to the U.S. military; by 1918, 80% of the state's industries were producing goods for the war effort.[[76]](#cite_note-77) [Remington Arms](/wiki/Remington_Arms "Remington Arms") in Bridgeport produced half the small-arms cartridges used by the U.S. Army,[[77]](#cite_note-78) with other major suppliers including [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company "Winchester Repeating Arms Company") in New Haven and [Colt](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company") in Hartford.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Dusen1961266–268-79)

Connecticut was also an important U.S. Navy supplier, with [Electric Boat](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Electric_Boat "General Dynamics Electric Boat") receiving orders for 85 submarines,[[79]](#cite_note-boat-80) [Lake Torpedo Boat](/wiki/Lake_Torpedo_Boat "Lake Torpedo Boat") building more than 20 subs,[[80]](#cite_note-81) and the [Groton Iron Works](/wiki/Groton_Iron_Works "Groton Iron Works") building freighters.[[81]](#cite_note-82) On June 21, 1916, the Navy made Groton the site for its East Coast submarine base and school.

The state enthusiastically supported the American war effort in 1917 and 1918 with large purchases of war bonds, a further expansion of industry, and an emphasis on increasing food production on the farms. Thousands of state, local, and volunteer groups mobilized for the war effort and were coordinated by the Connecticut State Council of Defense.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Manufacturers wrestled with manpower shortages; Waterbury's American Brass and Manufacturing Company was running at half capacity, so the federal government agreed to furlough soldiers to work there.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreen1997116-84)

#### Interwar period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Interwar period")]

In 1919, J. Henry Roraback started the [Connecticut Light & Power Co.](/wiki/Northeast_Utilities "Northeast Utilities")[[84]](#cite_note-85) which became the state's dominant electric utility. In 1925, [Frederick Rentschler](/wiki/Frederick_Rentschler "Frederick Rentschler") spurred the creation of [Pratt & Whitney](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney "Pratt & Whitney") in Hartford to develop engines for aircraft; the company became an important military supplier in World War II and one of the three major manufacturers of jet engines in the world.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

On September 21, 1938, the most destructive storm in New England history struck eastern Connecticut, killing hundreds of people.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The eye of the ["Long Island Express"](/wiki/1938_New_England_hurricane "1938 New England hurricane") passed just west of New Haven and devastated the Connecticut shoreline between Old Saybrook and Stonington from the full force of wind and waves, even though they had partial protection by Long Island. The hurricane caused extensive damage to infrastructure, homes, and businesses. In New London, a 500-foot (150 m) sailing ship was driven into a warehouse complex, causing a major fire. Heavy rainfall caused the Connecticut River to flood downtown Hartford and East Hartford. An estimated 50,000 trees fell onto roadways.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

#### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: World War II")]

The advent of [lend-lease](/wiki/Lend-lease "Lend-lease") in support of Britain helped lift Connecticut from the Great Depression,[[88]](#cite_note-89) with the state a major production center for weaponry and supplies used in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Connecticut manufactured 4.1% of total U.S. military armaments produced during the war, ranking ninth among the 48 states,[[89]](#cite_note-90) with major factories including [Colt](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company")[[90]](#cite_note-91) for firearms, [Pratt & Whitney](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney "Pratt & Whitney") for aircraft engines, [Chance Vought](/wiki/Vought "Vought") for fighter planes, [Hamilton Standard](/wiki/Hamilton_Standard "Hamilton Standard") for propellers,[[91]](#cite_note-92) and [Electric Boat](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Electric_Boat "General Dynamics Electric Boat") for submarines and PT boats.[[79]](#cite_note-boat-80)

On May 13, 1940, [Igor Sikorsky](/wiki/Igor_Sikorsky "Igor Sikorsky") made an untethered flight of the first practical [helicopter](/wiki/Helicopter "Helicopter").[[92]](#cite_note-93) The helicopter saw limited use in World War II, but future military production made [Sikorsky Aircraft](/wiki/Sikorsky_Aircraft "Sikorsky Aircraft")'s [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_Connecticut "Stratford, Connecticut") plant Connecticut's largest single manufacturing site by the start of the twenty-first century.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

#### Post-World War II economic expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Post-World War II economic expansion")]

Connecticut lost some wartime factories following the end of hostilities, but the state shared in a general post-war expansion that included the construction of highways[[94]](#cite_note-95) and resulting in middle-class growth in suburban areas.

[Prescott Bush](/wiki/Prescott_Bush "Prescott Bush") represented Connecticut in the U.S. Senate from 1952 to 1963; his son [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") and grandson [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") both became presidents of the United States.[[95]](#cite_note-96) In 1965, Connecticut ratified its [current constitution](/wiki/Connecticut_Constitution "Connecticut Constitution"), replacing the document that had served since 1818.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In 1968, commercial operation began for the Connecticut Yankee Nuclear Power Plant in [Haddam](/wiki/Haddam_Neck,_Connecticut "Haddam Neck, Connecticut"); in 1970, the [Millstone Nuclear Power Station](/wiki/Millstone_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Millstone Nuclear Power Plant") began operations in [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford,_Connecticut "Waterford, Connecticut").[[97]](#cite_note-98) In 1974, Connecticut elected Democratic Governor [Ella T. Grasso](/wiki/Ella_T._Grasso "Ella T. Grasso"), who became the first woman in any state to be elected governor without being the wife or widow of a previous governor.[[98]](#cite_note-Pumont-99)

#### Late twentieth century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Late twentieth century")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=). *(October 2021)* |

Connecticut's dependence on the defense industry posed an economic challenge at the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The resulting budget crisis helped elect [Lowell Weicker](/wiki/Lowell_Weicker "Lowell Weicker") as governor on a third-party ticket in 1990. Weicker's remedy was a state income tax which proved effective in balancing the budget, but only for the short-term. He did not run for a second term, in part because of this politically unpopular move.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

In 1992, initial construction was completed on [Foxwoods Casino](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") at the [Mashantucket Pequots](/wiki/Mashantucket_Pequots "Mashantucket Pequots") reservation in eastern Connecticut, which became the largest casino in the Western Hemisphere. [Mohegan Sun](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun "Mohegan Sun") followed four years later.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

### Early twenty-first century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Early twenty-first century")]

In 2000, presidential candidate [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") chose Senator [Joe Lieberman](/wiki/Joe_Lieberman "Joe Lieberman") as his running mate, marking the first time that a major party presidential ticket included someone of the Jewish faith.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Gore and Lieberman fell five votes short of [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Dick Cheney](/wiki/Dick_Cheney "Dick Cheney") in the Electoral College. In the terrorist [attacks of September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), 65 state residents were killed, mostly [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") residents who were working in the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973-2001) "World Trade Center (1973-2001)").[[102]](#cite_note-103) In 2004, Republican Governor [John G. Rowland](/wiki/John_G._Rowland "John G. Rowland") resigned during a corruption investigation, later pleading guilty to federal charges.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Connecticut was hit by three major storms in just over 14 months in 2011 and 2012, with all three causing extensive property damage and electric outages. [Hurricane Irene](/wiki/Hurricane_Irene "Hurricane Irene") struck Connecticut August 28, and damage totaled $235 million.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Two months later, the ["Halloween nor'easter"](/wiki/2011_Halloween_nor%27easter "2011 Halloween nor'easter") dropped extensive snow onto trees, resulting in snapped branches and trunks that damaged power lines; some areas were without electricity for 11 days.[[106]](#cite_note-107) [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") hit New Jersey and passed over Connecticut with hurricane-force winds and tides up to 12 feet above normal. Many coastal buildings were damaged or destroyed.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Sandy's winds drove storm surges into streets and cut power to 98% of homes and businesses, with more than $360 million in damage.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

On December 14, 2012, a 20-year-old man named [Adam Lanza](/wiki/Adam_Lanza "Adam Lanza") [shot and killed 26 people](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting") at [Sandy Hook Elementary School](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School "Sandy Hook Elementary School") in [Newtown](/wiki/Newtown,_Connecticut "Newtown, Connecticut"), and then killed himself.[[109]](#cite_note-110) The massacre spurred [renewed efforts](/wiki/Gun_control_after_the_Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Gun control after the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting") by activists for tighter laws on gun ownership nationally.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

In the summer and fall of 2016, Connecticut experienced a drought in many parts of the state, causing some water-use bans. As of November 15, 2016 (2016-11-15), 45% of the state was listed at Severe Drought by the U.S. Drought Monitor, including almost all of [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford_County,_Connecticut "Hartford County, Connecticut") and [Litchfield](/wiki/Litchfield_County,_Connecticut "Litchfield County, Connecticut") counties. All the rest of the state was in Moderate Drought or Severe Drought, including [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex_County,_Connecticut "Middlesex County, Connecticut"), [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut"), [New London](/wiki/New_London_County,_Connecticut "New London County, Connecticut"), [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven_County,_Connecticut "New Haven County, Connecticut"), [Windham](/wiki/Windham_County,_Connecticut "Windham County, Connecticut"), and [Tolland](/wiki/Tolland_County,_Connecticut "Tolland County, Connecticut") counties. This affected the agricultural economy in the state.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Connecticut](/wiki/Geography_of_Connecticut "Geography of Connecticut")

Further information: [Geography of New England](/wiki/Geography_of_New_England "Geography of New England")

Connecticut is bordered on the south by [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"), on the west by [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), on the north by [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), and on the east by [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"). The state capital and fourth largest city is [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford "Hartford"), and other major cities and towns (by population) include [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"), [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven "New Haven"), [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut"), [Waterbury](/wiki/Waterbury,_Connecticut "Waterbury, Connecticut"), [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk,_Connecticut "Norwalk, Connecticut"), [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury,_Connecticut "Danbury, Connecticut"), [New Britain](/wiki/New_Britain,_Connecticut "New Britain, Connecticut"), [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich,_Connecticut "Greenwich, Connecticut"), and [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_Connecticut "Bristol, Connecticut"). There are 169 [incorporated towns](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") in Connecticut, with cities and villages included within some towns.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Map_of_Connecticut_NA_cropped.png/330px-Map_of_Connecticut_NA_cropped.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Connecticut_NA_cropped.png)

Reference Map showing major highways and settlements

The highest peak in Connecticut is [Bear Mountain](/wiki/Bear_Mountain_(Connecticut) "Bear Mountain (Connecticut)") in [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_Connecticut "Salisbury, Connecticut") in the northwest corner of the state. The highest point is just east of where Connecticut, [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), and New York meet (42°3′ N, 73°29′ W), on the southern slope of [Mount Frissell](/wiki/Mount_Frissell "Mount Frissell"), whose peak lies nearby in Massachusetts.[[115]](#cite_note-116) At the opposite extreme, many of the coastal towns have areas that are less than 20 feet (6.1 m) above sea level.

Connecticut has a long maritime history and a reputation based on that history—yet the state has no direct oceanfront (technically speaking). The coast of Connecticut sits on [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"), which is an [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary"). The state's access to the open Atlantic Ocean is both to the west (toward New York City) and to the east (toward the "race" near Rhode Island). Due to this unique geography, Long Island Sound and the Connecticut shoreline are relatively protected from high waves from storms.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

The [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") cuts through the center of the state, flowing into Long Island Sound. The most populous metropolitan region centered within the state lies in the [Connecticut River Valley](/wiki/Connecticut_River_Valley "Connecticut River Valley"). Despite Connecticut's relatively small size, it features wide regional variations in its landscape; for example, in the northwestern [Litchfield Hills](/wiki/Litchfield_Hills "Litchfield Hills"), it features rolling mountains and horse farms, whereas in areas to the east of New Haven along the coast, the landscape features coastal [marshes](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh"), [beaches](/wiki/Beach "Beach"), and large scale maritime activities.

Connecticut's rural areas and small towns in the northeast and northwest corners of the state contrast sharply with its industrial cities such as Stamford, Bridgeport, and New Haven, located along the coastal highways from the New York border to New London, then northward up the Connecticut River to Hartford. Many towns in northeastern and northwestern Connecticut center around a [green](/wiki/Village_green "Village green"). Near the green typically stand historical visual symbols of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") towns, such as a white [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)"), a [colonial meeting house](/wiki/Colonial_meeting_house "Colonial meeting house"), a colonial [tavern](/wiki/Tavern "Tavern") or [inn](/wiki/Inn "Inn"), several [colonial houses](/wiki/American_colonial_architecture "American colonial architecture"), and so on, establishing a scenic historical appearance maintained for both [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") and tourism. Many of the areas in southern and coastal Connecticut have been built up and rebuilt over the years, and look less visually like traditional New England.

The northern boundary of the state with Massachusetts is marked by the [Southwick Jog](/wiki/Southwick_Jog "Southwick Jog") or Granby Notch, an approximately 2.5 miles (4.0 km) square detour into Connecticut. The origin of this anomaly is clearly established in a long line of disputes and temporary agreements which were finally concluded in 1804, when southern [Southwick's](/wiki/Southwick,_Massachusetts "Southwick, Massachusetts") residents sought to leave Massachusetts, and the town was split in half.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)

The southwestern border of Connecticut where it abuts New York State is marked by a [panhandle](/wiki/Salient_(geography) "Salient (geography)") in [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") and the [Western Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/Western_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "Western Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut"), containing the towns of [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich,_Connecticut "Greenwich, Connecticut"), [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut"), [New Canaan](/wiki/New_Canaan,_Connecticut "New Canaan, Connecticut"), [Darien](/wiki/Darien,_Connecticut "Darien, Connecticut"), and parts of [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk,_Connecticut "Norwalk, Connecticut") and [Wilton](/wiki/Wilton,_Connecticut "Wilton, Connecticut"). This irregularity in the boundary is the result of [territorial disputes](/wiki/History_of_Connecticut#Territorial_disputes "History of Connecticut") in the late 17th century, culminating with New York giving up its claim to the area, whose residents considered themselves part of Connecticut, in exchange for an equivalent area extending northwards from [Ridgefield](/wiki/Ridgefield,_Connecticut "Ridgefield, Connecticut") to the Massachusetts border, as well as undisputed claim to [Rye, New York](/wiki/Rye_(town),_New_York "Rye (town), New York").[[119]](#cite_note-120)

Areas maintained by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") include [Appalachian National Scenic Trail](/wiki/Appalachian_National_Scenic_Trail "Appalachian National Scenic Trail"), [Quinebaug and Shetucket Rivers Valley National Heritage Corridor](/wiki/Quinebaug_and_Shetucket_Rivers_Valley_National_Heritage_Corridor "Quinebaug and Shetucket Rivers Valley National Heritage Corridor"), and [Weir Farm National Historic Site](/wiki/Weir_Farm_National_Historic_Site "Weir Farm National Historic Site").[[120]](#cite_note-121)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Climate")]

Further information: [Climate change in Connecticut](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Connecticut "Climate change in Connecticut")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Connecticut.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Connecticut.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Connecticut.png)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Connecticut, using 1991–2020 [climate normals](/wiki/Climatological_normal "Climatological normal").

Connecticut lies at the rough transition zone between the southern end of the [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") and the northern portion of the [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"). Northern Connecticut generally experiences a climate with hot, humid summers and moderately cold winters with periodic snowfall. Far southern and coastal Connecticut has a climate with cool winters with a mix of rain and infrequent snow, and the long hot and humid summers typical of the middle and lower [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"). Coastal Connecticut is the very broad transition zone between the humid continental climate and the humid subtropical climate.

#### Precipitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Precipitation")]

Connecticut sees a fairly even precipitation pattern spread throughout the 12 months.[[121]](#cite_note-:2-122) Connecticut averages 56% of possible sunshine (higher than the U.S. national average), averaging 2,400 hours of [sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine "Sunshine") annually.[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-:2-122) Occasionally, some months may see extremes in precipitation, either much higher or lower than normal, though long term droughts and floods are rare.[[121]](#cite_note-:2-122)

Early spring can range from slightly cool (40s to low 50s F) to warm (65 to 70 F), while mid and late spring (late April/May) is warm. By late May, the building [Bermuda High](/wiki/Bermuda_High "Bermuda High") creates a southerly flow of warm and humid tropical air, bringing hot weather conditions throughout the state. Average highs are 81 °F (27 °C) in New London and 85 °F (29 °C) in Windsor Locks at the peak of summer in late July. On occasion, heat waves with highs from 90 to 100 °F (38 °C) occur across Connecticut. Connecticut's record high temperature is 106 °F (41 °C) which occurred in [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury,_Connecticut "Danbury, Connecticut") on July 15, 1995.[[123]](#cite_note-:3-124) Although summers are sunny in Connecticut, quick moving summer thunderstorms can bring brief downpours with thunder and lightning. Occasionally these thunderstorms can be severe, and the state usually averages one tornado per year.[[124]](#cite_note-tornadoes-125) During hurricane season, the remains of tropical cyclones occasionally affect the region, though a direct hit is rare. Some notable hurricanes to impact the state include the [1938 New England hurricane](/wiki/1938_New_England_hurricane "1938 New England hurricane"), [Hurricane Carol](/wiki/Hurricane_Carol "Hurricane Carol") in 1954, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") in 2012, and [Hurricane Isaias](/wiki/Hurricane_Isaias "Hurricane Isaias") in 2020.[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127)

Weather commonly associated with the fall season typically begins in October and lasts to the first days of December. Daily high temperatures in October and November range from the 50s to 60s F. Winters (December through mid-March) are moderately generally cold from south to north in Connecticut. The coldest month (January) has average high temperatures ranging from 38 °F (3 °C) in the coastal lowlands to 33 °F (1 °C) in the inland and northern portions on the state.

The lowest temperature recorded in Connecticut is −32 °F (−36 °C) which has been observed twice: in [Falls Village](/wiki/Falls_Village,_Connecticut "Falls Village, Connecticut") on February 16, 1943, and in [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry,_Connecticut "Coventry, Connecticut") on January 22, 1961.[[123]](#cite_note-:3-124) The average yearly snowfall ranges from about 60 inches (1,500 mm) in the higher elevations of the northern portion of the state to only 20–25 inches (510–640 mm) along the southeast coast of Connecticut (Branford to Groton). Most of Connecticut has less than 60 days of snow cover, while coastal areas often only see 30 days or so of snowcover. Annually, 95% of seasonal snowfall in Connecticut falls from early December to late March. On occasion in winter, Connecticut can occasionally get heavy snowstorms, called [nor'easters](/wiki/Nor%27easter "Nor'easter"), which may produce as much as two feet of snow on rare occasions.[[121]](#cite_note-:2-122)[[127]](#cite_note-128) Although rare, [ice storms](/wiki/Ice_storm "Ice storm") also occur on occasion, such as the [Southern New England ice storm of 1973](/wiki/Southern_New_England_ice_storm_of_1973 "Southern New England ice storm of 1973").

### Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Flora")]

Main article: [Flora of Connecticut](/wiki/Flora_of_Connecticut "Flora of Connecticut")

See also: [List of Connecticut tree species](/wiki/List_of_Connecticut_tree_species "List of Connecticut tree species")

Forests consist of a mix of [Northeastern coastal forests](/wiki/Northeastern_coastal_forests "Northeastern coastal forests") of oak in southern areas of the state, to the upland [New England-Acadian forests](/wiki/New_England-Acadian_forests "New England-Acadian forests") in the northwestern parts of the state. Mountain Laurel (*[Kalmia latifolia](/wiki/Kalmia_latifolia "Kalmia latifolia")*) is the state flower and is native to low ridges in several parts of Connecticut. Rosebay rhododendron (*[Rhododendron maximum](/wiki/Rhododendron_maximum "Rhododendron maximum")*) is also native to eastern uplands of Connecticut and [Pachaug State Forest](/wiki/Pachaug_State_Forest "Pachaug State Forest") is home to the Rhododendron Sanctuary Trail. Atlantic white cedar (*[Chamaecyparis thyoides](/wiki/Chamaecyparis_thyoides "Chamaecyparis thyoides")*), is found in wetlands in the southern parts of the state. Connecticut has one native cactus (*[Opuntia humifusa](/wiki/Opuntia_humifusa "Opuntia humifusa")*), found in sandy coastal areas and low hillsides. Several types of beach grasses and wildflowers are also native to Connecticut.[[130]](#cite_note-131) Connecticut spans [USDA Plant Hardiness Zones](/wiki/USDA_Plant_Hardiness_Zones "USDA Plant Hardiness Zones") 5b to 7a. Coastal Connecticut is the broad transition zone where more southern and subtropical plants are cultivated.

As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Connecticut has a population of 3,605,944, an increase of 31,847 people (0.9%) from the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[134]](#cite_note-:0-135) Among the census records, 20.4% of the population was under 18.

In 1790, 97% of the population in Connecticut was classified as "rural". The first census in which less than half the population was classified as rural was 1890. In the 2000 census, only 12.3% was considered rural. Most of western and southern Connecticut (particularly the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(Connecticut) "Gold Coast (Connecticut)")) is strongly associated with New York City; this area is the most affluent and populous region of the state and has high property costs and high incomes. The [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") of Connecticut is located in the town of [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire,_Connecticut "Cheshire, Connecticut").[[135]](#cite_note-NGS-136)

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 2,930 [homeless people in Connecticut](/wiki/Homelessness_in_Connecticut "Homelessness in Connecticut").[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138)

In common with the majority of the United States, non-Hispanic whites have remained the dominant racial and ethnic group in Connecticut. From being 98% of the population in 1940, however, they have declined to 63% of the population as of the 2020 census.[[134]](#cite_note-:0-135)[[139]](#cite_note-Race2000-141) These statistics have represented fewer Americans identifying as non-Hispanic white, which has given rise to the Hispanic and Latino American population and Asian American population overall.[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), 46.1% of Connecticut's population younger than age 1 were minorities.[[145]](#cite_note-147) As of 2004, 11.4% of the population (400,000) was foreign-born. In 1870, native-born Americans had accounted for 75% of the state's population, but that had dropped to 35% by 1918. Also as of 2000, 81.69% of Connecticut residents age 5 and older spoke English at home and 8.42% spoke Spanish, followed by Italian at 1.59%, French at 1.31%, and Polish at 1.20%.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

The largest ancestry groups since 2010 were:[[147]](#cite_note-149) 19.3% [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), 17.9% [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"), 10.7% [English](/wiki/English_American "English American"), 10.4% [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), 8.6% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American"), 6.6% [French](/wiki/Franco_American "Franco American"), 3.0% [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian"), 2.7% [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans"), 2.0% [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_American "Scottish American"), and 1.4% [Scotch Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_American "Scotch-Irish American").

[Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanics_in_the_United_States "Hispanics in the United States") in Connecticut make up 19.2% of the population, predominantly of [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Stateside_Puerto_Ricans "Stateside Puerto Ricans") (8.2%) origin, followed by [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") (1.7%) and [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans") (1.5%). The state has the highest share of Puerto Ricans in the country and the sixth largest Puerto Rican population (301,931) in the country.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

Connecticut is one of the most racially [segregated](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States") states in the nation, with the nonwhite population largely concentrated in major urban areas such as Bridgeport, Hartford, New Haven and Waterbury. In many cases, towns neighboring urban areas are sharply segregated from them.[[149]](#cite_note-:4-151)

The top countries of origin for Connecticut's immigrants in 2018 were [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador").[[150]](#cite_note-152)
