### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Religion")]

A 2014 Pew survey of Connecticut residents' religious self-identification showed the following distribution of affiliations[[162]](#cite_note-pew2014-164) Protestant 35%, [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Roman Catholicism in the United States") 33%, [non-religious](/wiki/Non-religious "Non-religious") 28%, Jewish 3%, [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism") 1%, Orthodox 1%, [Jehovah's Witness](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") 1%, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") 1%, [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") 1% and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") 1%.[[162]](#cite_note-pew2014-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165) [Jewish congregations](/wiki/Jewish_American "Jewish American") had 108,280 (3.2%) members in 2000.[[164]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com-166)

The Jewish population is concentrated in the towns near Long Island Sound between [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich,_Connecticut "Greenwich, Connecticut") and [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"), in [Greater New Haven](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven") and in [Greater Hartford](/wiki/Greater_Hartford "Greater Hartford"), especially the suburb of [West Hartford](/wiki/West_Hartford,_Connecticut "West Hartford, Connecticut"). According to the [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives"), the largest Christian denominations, by number of adherents, in 2010 were: the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), with 1,252,936; the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), with 96,506; and non-denominational Evangelical Protestants, with 72,863.[[164]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com-166)

Recent immigration has brought other non-Christian religions to the state, but the numbers of adherents of other religions are still low. Connecticut is also home to New England's largest Protestant church: [The First Cathedral](/wiki/The_First_Cathedral "The First Cathedral") in [Bloomfield, Connecticut](/wiki/Bloomfield,_Connecticut "Bloomfield, Connecticut"). Hartford is seat to the Roman Catholic [Archdiocese of Hartford](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Hartford "Archdiocese of Hartford"), which is sovereign over the [Diocese of Bridgeport](/wiki/Diocese_of_Bridgeport "Diocese of Bridgeport") and the [Diocese of Norwich](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Norwich "Roman Catholic Diocese of Norwich").

By a 2020 [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") survey, 71% of the population identified as some form of Christian.[[165]](#cite_note-167) It found the state to be 21% [non-religious](/wiki/Non-religious "Non-religious") and specifically 19% white [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), 19% white Catholic, 9% white evangelical Protestant, 7% black Protestant, and 7% Hispanic Catholic. In contrast to the 2014 Pew survey,[[c]](#cite_note-168) the 2020 PRRI survey found Connecticut to be 40% Protestant and 28% Catholic (with the remainder of Christians being Mormon at 2%, and Orthodox at 1%). The PRRI survey found Jewish citizens to be 2% of the population and, like the Pew survey: Hindus, Buddhists, and Muslims to be 1% each.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of Connecticut](/wiki/Economy_of_Connecticut "Economy of Connecticut") and [List of Connecticut locations by per capita income](/wiki/List_of_Connecticut_locations_by_per_capita_income "List of Connecticut locations by per capita income")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Connecticut_Welcomes_You_Sign.jpg/250px-Connecticut_Welcomes_You_Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Connecticut_Welcomes_You_Sign.jpg)

A welcome sign on I-91 in [Enfield](/wiki/Enfield,_Connecticut "Enfield, Connecticut").

The total 2024 [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") for Connecticut was $365.7 billion, up from $345.9 billion in 2023.[[166]](#cite_note-169)

Connecticut's [adjusted per capita personal income](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_adjusted_per_capita_personal_income "List of U.S. states by adjusted per capita personal income") in 2022 was estimated at $77,940, third-highest among states.[[167]](#cite_note-170) There is a large disparity in incomes throughout the state; Connecticut was tied with California and Massachusetts for the second highest (after New York's 0.52) [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient"), at 0.50, as of 2020.[[168]](#cite_note-171) As of 2025, it remained tied for second with Louisiana, with only New York having higher levels of inequality. Despite its overall inequality, Connecticut has a relatively low poverty rate.[[149]](#cite_note-:4-151) According to a 2018 study by Phoenix Marketing International, Connecticut had the third-largest number of millionaires per capita in the United States, with a ratio of 7.75%.[[169]](#cite_note-172) [New Canaan](/wiki/New_Canaan,_Connecticut "New Canaan, Connecticut") is the wealthiest town in Connecticut, with a per capita income of $105,846. [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") is the poorest municipality in Connecticut, with a per capita income of $16,798 in 2020.[[170]](#cite_note-20Cen-173) [At the county level](/wiki/List_of_Connecticut_locations_by_per_capita_income#Counties "List of Connecticut locations by per capita income"), per capita income ranged from $48,295 in [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") to $26,585 in [Windham County](/wiki/Windham_County,_Connecticut "Windham County, Connecticut"), which is close to the United States average.[[170]](#cite_note-20Cen-173)

As of May 2025, Connecticut's unemployment rate was 3.8%,[[171]](#cite_note-174) with U.S. unemployment at 4.2% that month.[[172]](#cite_note-175) Dating back to 1982, Connecticut recorded its lowest unemployment in 2000 between August and October, at 2.2%. The highest unemployment rate during that period occurred in November and December 2010 at 9.3%,[[173]](#cite_note-176) but economists expected record new levels of layoffs as a result of business closures in the spring of 2020 due to the coronavirus pandemic.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Taxation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Taxation")]

Tax is collected by the Connecticut Department of Revenue Services[[175]](#cite_note-178) and by local municipalities.[[176]](#cite_note-State_of_Connecticut-179)

As of 2012, Connecticut residents had the second highest rate in the nation of combined state and local taxes after New York, at 12.6% of income compared to the national average of 9.9% as reported by the [Tax Foundation](/wiki/Tax_Foundation "Tax Foundation").[[177]](#cite_note-180)

Before 1991, Connecticut had an investment-only [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") system. Income from employment was untaxed, but income from investments was taxed at 13%, the highest rate in the U.S., with no deductions allowed for costs of producing the investment income, such as interest on borrowing.

In 1991, under Governor [Lowell P. Weicker Jr.](/wiki/Lowell_P._Weicker_Jr. "Lowell P. Weicker Jr."), an independent, the system was changed to one in which the taxes on employment income and investment income were equalized at a maximum rate of 4%. The new tax policy drew investment firms to Connecticut; as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), [Fairfield County](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") was home to the headquarters for 16 of the 200 largest [hedge funds](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") in the world.[[178]](#cite_note-181)

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), the income tax rates on Connecticut individuals were divided into seven tax brackets of 3% (on income up to $10,000); 5% ($10,000–$50,000); 5.5% ($50,000–$100,000); 6% ($100,000–$200,000); 6.5% ($200,000–$250,000); 6.9% ($250,000–$500,000); and 6.99% above $500,000, with additional amounts owed depending on the bracket.[[179]](#cite_note-182)

All wages of Connecticut residents are subject to the state's income tax, even if earned outside the state. However, in those cases, Connecticut income tax must be withheld only to the extent the Connecticut tax exceeds the amount withheld by the other jurisdiction.[[180]](#cite_note-Resident_Working_in_Another_State-183) Since New York has higher income tax rates than Connecticut,[[181]](#cite_note-184) this effectively means that Connecticut residents who work in New York have no Connecticut income tax withheld. Connecticut permits a credit for taxes paid to other jurisdictions, but since residents who work in other states are still subject to Connecticut income taxation, they may owe taxes if the jurisdictional credit does not fully offset the Connecticut tax amount.[[180]](#cite_note-Resident_Working_in_Another_State-183)

Connecticut levies a 6.35% state [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") on the retail sale, lease, or rental of most goods.[[182]](#cite_note-2011Sect6Summary-185) Some items and services in general are not subject to sales and use taxes unless specifically enumerated as taxable by [statute](/wiki/Statute "Statute"). A provision excluding clothing under $50 from sales tax was repealed as of July 1, 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit).[[182]](#cite_note-2011Sect6Summary-185) There are no additional sales taxes imposed by local jurisdictions. In 2001, Connecticut instituted what became an annual sales tax "holiday" each August lasting one week, when retailers do not have to remit sales tax on certain items and quantities of clothing that has varied from year to year.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

State law authorizes municipalities to tax property, including real estate, vehicles and other personal property, with state statute providing varying exemptions, credits and abatements. All assessments are at 70% of [fair market value](/wiki/Fair_market_value "Fair market value").[[176]](#cite_note-State_of_Connecticut-179) The maximum property tax credit is $200 per return[[184]](#cite_note-187) and any excess may not be refunded or carried forward.[[185]](#cite_note-inctax-188) According to the Tax Foundation, on a per capita basis in the 2017 fiscal year Connecticut residents paid the 3rd highest average property taxes in the nation after [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") and New Jersey.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

As of January 1, 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), gasoline taxes and fees in Connecticut were 40.13 cents per gallon, 11th highest in the United States which had a nationwide average of 36.13 cents a gallon excluding federal taxes. Diesel taxes and fees as of January 2020 in Connecticut were 46.50 cents per gallon, ninth highest nationally with the U.S. average at 37.91 cents.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

### Real estate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Real estate")]

In 2019, sales of single-family homes in Connecticut totaled 33,146 units, a 2.1 percent decline from the 2018 transaction total. The median home sold in 2019 recorded a transaction amount of $260,000, up 0.4 percent from 2018.[[188]](#cite_note-191)

Connecticut had the seventh highest rate of home foreclosure activity in the country in 2019 at 0.53 percent of the total housing stock.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

### Industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Industries")]

See also: [List of Connecticut companies](/wiki/List_of_Connecticut_companies "List of Connecticut companies")

Finance, insurance and real estate was Connecticut's largest industry in 2018 as ranked by gross domestic product, generating $75.7 billion in GDP that year.[[190]](#cite_note-auto1-193) Major employers include [The Hartford](/wiki/The_Hartford "The Hartford"), [Travelers](/wiki/The_Travelers_Companies "The Travelers Companies"), [Harman International](/wiki/Harman_International "Harman International"), [Cigna](/wiki/Cigna "Cigna"), the [Aetna](/wiki/Aetna "Aetna") subsidiary of CVS Health, [Mass Mutual](/wiki/Massachusetts_Mutual_Life_Insurance_Company "Massachusetts Mutual Life Insurance Company"), [People's United Financial](/wiki/People%27s_United_Bank "People's United Bank"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [Realogy](/wiki/Realogy "Realogy"),[[191]](#cite_note-employers-194) [Bridgewater Associates](/wiki/Bridgewater_Associates "Bridgewater Associates"),[[192]](#cite_note-195) [GE Capital](/wiki/GE_Capital "GE Capital"),[[193]](#cite_note-196) William Raveis Real Estate,[[194]](#cite_note-Connecticut_Department_of_Labor-197) and [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") through reinsurance and residential real estate subsidiaries.

The combined educational, health and social services sector was the largest single industry as ranked by employment, with a combined workforce of 342,600 people at the end of 2019,[[195]](#cite_note-198) ranking fourth the year before in GDP at $28.3 billion.

The broad business and professional services sector had the second highest GDP total in Connecticut in 2018 at an estimated $33.7 billion.[[190]](#cite_note-auto1-193)

Manufacturing was the third biggest industry in 2018 with GDP of $30.8 billion,[[190]](#cite_note-auto1-193) dominated by [Raytheon Technologies](/wiki/Raytheon_Technologies "Raytheon Technologies") formed in the March 2020 merger of Hartford-based [United Technologies](/wiki/United_Technologies "United Technologies") and Waltham, Mass.-based Raytheon Co. As of the merger, Raytheon Technologies employed about 19,000 people in Connecticut through subsidiaries [Pratt & Whitney](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney "Pratt & Whitney") and [Collins Aerospace](/wiki/Collins_Aerospace "Collins Aerospace").[[196]](#cite_note-199) [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin") subsidiary [Sikorsky Aircraft](/wiki/Sikorsky_Aircraft "Sikorsky Aircraft") operates Connecticut's single largest manufacturing plant in [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford,_Connecticut "Stratford, Connecticut"),[[194]](#cite_note-Connecticut_Department_of_Labor-197) where it makes helicopters.

Major audio equipment manufacturing company [Harman International](/wiki/Harman_International "Harman International") is headquartered in Stamford, Connecticut.[[197]](#cite_note-200) It owns many brands like [JBL](/wiki/JBL "JBL"), [Akg](/wiki/AKG_(company) "AKG (company)") and [Harman kardon](/wiki/Harman_kardon "Harman kardon").[[198]](#cite_note-201)

Other major manufacturers include the [Electric Boat](/wiki/Electric_Boat "Electric Boat") division of [General Dynamics](/wiki/General_Dynamics "General Dynamics"), which makes submarines in [Groton](/wiki/Groton,_Connecticut "Groton, Connecticut").[[79]](#cite_note-boat-80)

Connecticut historically was a center of gun manufacturing, and four gun-manufacturing firms continued to operate in the state as of December 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), employing 2,000 people: [Colt](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company"), [Stag](/wiki/Stag_Arms "Stag Arms"), [Ruger](/wiki/Sturm,_Ruger_%26_Co. "Sturm, Ruger & Co."), and [Mossberg](/wiki/O.F._Mossberg_%26_Sons "O.F. Mossberg & Sons").[[199]](#cite_note-NYT122312-202) [Marlin](/wiki/Marlin_Firearms "Marlin Firearms"), owned by [Remington](/wiki/Remington_Arms "Remington Arms"), closed in April 2011.[[200]](#cite_note-Courant010111-203)

Other large components of the Connecticut economy in 2018 included wholesale trade ($18.1 billion in GDP); information services ($13.8 billion); retail ($13.7 billion); arts, entertainment and food services ($9.1 billion); and construction ($8.3 billion).[[190]](#cite_note-auto1-193)

Tourists spent $9.3 billion in Connecticut in 2017 according to estimates as part of a series of studies commissioned by the state of Connecticut.[[201]](#cite_note-204) [Foxwoods Resort Casino](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") and [Mohegan Sun](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun "Mohegan Sun") are the two biggest tourist draws and number among the state's largest employers;[[191]](#cite_note-employers-194) both are located on Native American reservations in the southeastern Connecticut.

Main article: [Agriculture in Connecticut](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Connecticut "Agriculture in Connecticut")

Connecticut's agricultural production totaled $580 million in 2017, with just over half of that revenue the result of [nursery stock](/wiki/Nursery_(horticulture) "Nursery (horticulture)") production. Milk production totaled $81 million that year, with other major product categories including [eggs](/wiki/Egg_(food) "Egg (food)"), [vegetables](/wiki/Vegetables "Vegetables") and [fruit](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"), [tobacco](/wiki/Connecticut_shade_tobacco "Connecticut shade tobacco") and [shellfish](/wiki/Shellfish "Shellfish").[[202]](#cite_note-205)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Energy")]

See also: [List of power stations in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Connecticut "List of power stations in Connecticut")

Connecticut's economy uses less energy to produce each dollar of GDP than all other states except California, Massachusetts, and New York. It uses less energy on a per-capita basis than all but six other states. It has no fossil-fuel resources, but does have renewable resources. Average retail electricity prices are the highest among the 48 contiguous states. While most of the state's energy consumption is generated using fossil fuels, [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") delivered over 40% of state's electricity generation in 2019. [Refuse-derived fuels](/wiki/Refuse-derived_fuel "Refuse-derived fuel") and other [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") provided the largest share of renewable electricity at about a 3% share. Solar and wind generation have grown in recent years. More than three-quarters of solar generation came from distributed small-scale installations such as rooftop solar in 2019, and there is planning underway to significantly increase renewable generation with the state's offshore wind resource.[[203]](#cite_note-206)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transportation in Connecticut](/wiki/Transportation_in_Connecticut "Transportation in Connecticut")

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Roads")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of State Routes in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_State_Routes_in_Connecticut "List of State Routes in Connecticut").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red%2C_White%2C_and_Blue_%2827460771747%29.jpg/250px-Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red%2C_White%2C_and_Blue_%2827460771747%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red,_White,_and_Blue_(27460771747).jpg)

The [Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_Memorial_Bridge_(Connecticut) "Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge (Connecticut)"), locally known as the **Q Bridge**, carries ten lanes over the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River") in [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"), along the [Connecticut Turnpike](/wiki/Connecticut_Turnpike "Connecticut Turnpike").

The [Interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_highway "Interstate highway") in the state are [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Connecticut "Interstate 95 in Connecticut") (I-95) traveling southwest to northeast along the coast, [I-84](/wiki/Interstate_84_in_Connecticut "Interstate 84 in Connecticut") traveling southwest to northeast in the center of the state, [I-91](/wiki/Interstate_91_in_Connecticut "Interstate 91 in Connecticut") traveling north to south in the center of the state, and [I-395](/wiki/Interstate_395_in_Connecticut "Interstate 395 in Connecticut") traveling north to south near the eastern border of the state. The other major highways in Connecticut are the [Merritt Parkway](/wiki/Merritt_Parkway "Merritt Parkway") and [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway"), which together form [Connecticut Route 15](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_15 "Connecticut Route 15") (Route 15), traveling from the [Hutchinson River Parkway](/wiki/Hutchinson_River_Parkway "Hutchinson River Parkway") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") parallel to I-95 before turning north of [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven "New Haven") and traveling parallel to I-91, finally becoming a surface road in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_Connecticut "Berlin, Connecticut"). I-95 and Route 15 were originally [toll roads](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road"); they relied on a system of [toll plazas](/wiki/Toll_plaza "Toll plaza") at which all traffic stopped and paid fixed tolls. A series of major crashes at these plazas eventually contributed to the decision to remove the tolls in 1988.[[204]](#cite_note-207) Other major arteries in the state include [U.S. Route 7](/wiki/U.S._Route_7_in_Connecticut "U.S. Route 7 in Connecticut") (US 7) in the west traveling parallel to the New York state line, [Route 8](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_8 "Connecticut Route 8") farther east near the industrial city of Waterbury and traveling north–south along the Naugatuck River Valley nearly parallel with US 7, and [Route 9](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_9 "Connecticut Route 9") in the east.

Between New Haven and New York City, I-95 is one of the most congested highways in the United States. Although I-95 has been widened in several spots, some areas are only three lanes and this strains traffic capacity, resulting in frequent and lengthy [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour") delays. Frequently, the congestion spills over to clog the parallel Merritt Parkway and even US 1. The state has encouraged traffic reduction schemes, including rail use and [ride-sharing](/wiki/Carpool "Carpool").[[205]](#cite_note-208)

Connecticut also has a very active bicycling community, with one of the highest rates of bicycle ownership and use in the United States, particularly in New Haven. According to the U.S. Census 2006 American Community Survey, New Haven has the highest percentage of commuters who bicycle to work of any major metropolitan center on the East Coast.[[206]](#cite_note-209)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Metro-North_train_1567_enters_Stamford.jpg/250px-Metro-North_train_1567_enters_Stamford.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metro-North_train_1567_enters_Stamford.jpg)

A Metro-North Railroad New Haven Line train leaving [Stamford Station](/wiki/Stamford_Transportation_Center "Stamford Transportation Center")

Rail is a popular travel mode between New Haven and New York City's [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"). Southwestern Connecticut is served by the [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad")'s [New Haven Line](/wiki/New_Haven_Line "New Haven Line"), operated by the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority"). Metro-North provides commuter service between New York City and New Haven, with branches to [New Canaan](/wiki/New_Canaan_Branch "New Canaan Branch"), [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury_Branch "Danbury Branch"), and [Waterbury](/wiki/Waterbury_Branch "Waterbury Branch"). Connecticut lies along [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor"), which features frequent [Northeast Regional](/wiki/Northeast_Regional "Northeast Regional") and [Acela Express](/wiki/Acela_Express "Acela Express") service from New Haven south to New York City, Philadelphia, Baltimore, Washington, D.C., and Norfolk, VA, as well as north to [New London](/wiki/New_London,_Connecticut "New London, Connecticut"), [Providence](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island") and Boston. Since 1990, coastal cities and towns between New Haven and New London are also served by the [Shore Line East](/wiki/Shore_Line_East "Shore Line East") commuter line.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

In June 2018, a commuter rail service called the [Hartford Line](/wiki/Hartford_Line "Hartford Line") began operating between New Haven and Springfield on Amtrak's [New Haven-Springfield Line](/wiki/New_Haven-Springfield_Line "New Haven-Springfield Line").[[208]](#cite_note-211) Hartford Line service is provided by both Amtrak and the Connecticut Department of Transportation's [CT Rail](/wiki/CT_Rail "CT Rail"), and in addition to its termini serves [New Haven State Street](/wiki/New_Haven_State_Street_station "New Haven State Street station"), [Wallingford](/wiki/Wallingford_station_(Connecticut) "Wallingford station (Connecticut)"), [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden_Transit_Center "Meriden Transit Center"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin_station_(Connecticut) "Berlin station (Connecticut)"), [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford_Union_Station "Hartford Union Station"), [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_station_(Connecticut) "Windsor station (Connecticut)"), and [Windsor Locks](/wiki/Windsor_Locks_station "Windsor Locks station"). Several infill stations are planned to be added in the near future as of 2021. Amtrak's [Vermonter](/wiki/Vermonter_(train) "Vermonter (train)") runs from Washington to St. Albans, Vermont via the same line. In July 2019, Amtrak launched the *[Valley Flyer](/wiki/Valley_Flyer "Valley Flyer")*, which runs between New Haven and [Greenfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Greenfield,_Massachusetts "Greenfield, Massachusetts").[[209]](#cite_note-212)

A proposed commuter rail service, the [Central Corridor Rail Line](/wiki/Central_Corridor_Rail_Line "Central Corridor Rail Line"), would connect New London with [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich,_CT "Norwich, CT"), [Willimantic](/wiki/Willimantic,_CT "Willimantic, CT"), [Storrs](/wiki/Storrs,_CT "Storrs, CT") via the main campus of the [University of Connecticut](/wiki/University_of_Connecticut "University of Connecticut"), and [Stafford Springs](/wiki/Stafford_Springs,_CT "Stafford Springs, CT"), with service continuing into [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") and [Brattleboro, Vermont](/wiki/Brattleboro,_Vermont "Brattleboro, Vermont").[[210]](#cite_note-213) The proposal also adds stops to service popular tourist destinations [Foxwoods Resort Casino](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") and [Mohegan Sun](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun "Mohegan Sun").

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Bus")]

Statewide [bus](/wiki/Bus "Bus") service is supplied by [Connecticut Transit](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit "Connecticut Transit"), owned by the [Connecticut Department of Transportation](/wiki/Connecticut_Department_of_Transportation "Connecticut Department of Transportation"), with smaller municipal authorities providing local service. Bus networks are an important part of the transportation system in Connecticut, especially in urban areas like Hartford, Stamford, Norwalk, Bridgeport and New Haven. Connecticut Transit also operates [CTfastrak](/wiki/CTfastrak "CTfastrak"), a [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") service between [New Britain](/wiki/New_Britain,_Connecticut "New Britain, Connecticut") and [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), which opened to the public on March 28, 2015.[[211]](#cite_note-march28-214)[[212]](#cite_note-CTfastrak-215)[[213]](#cite_note-216)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Florida_148.jpg/250px-Florida_148.jpg)](/wiki/File:Florida_148.jpg)

[Bradley International Airport](/wiki/Bradley_International_Airport "Bradley International Airport"), the state's largest

Connecticut's largest airport is [Bradley International Airport](/wiki/Bradley_International_Airport "Bradley International Airport") in [Windsor Locks](/wiki/Windsor_Locks "Windsor Locks"), 15 miles (24 km) north of [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut").[[214]](#cite_note-217) Many residents of central and southern Connecticut also make heavy use of [JFK International Airport](/wiki/JFK_International_Airport "JFK International Airport") and [Newark International Airports](/wiki/Newark_International_Airport "Newark International Airport"), especially for international travel. Smaller regional air service is provided at [Tweed New Haven Regional Airport](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Regional_Airport "Tweed New Haven Regional Airport"). Larger civil airports include [Danbury Municipal Airport](/wiki/Danbury_Municipal_Airport "Danbury Municipal Airport") and [Waterbury-Oxford Airport](/wiki/Waterbury-Oxford_Airport "Waterbury-Oxford Airport") in western Connecticut, [Hartford–Brainard Airport](/wiki/Hartford%E2%80%93Brainard_Airport "Hartford–Brainard Airport") in central Connecticut, and [Groton-New London Airport](/wiki/Groton-New_London_Airport "Groton-New London Airport") in eastern Connecticut. [Sikorsky Memorial Airport](/wiki/Sikorsky_Memorial_Airport "Sikorsky Memorial Airport") is located in Stratford and mostly services cargo, helicopter and private aviation.

### Ferry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Ferry")]

Several ferry services cross Long Island Sound and connect the state to [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"). The [Bridgeport & Port Jefferson Ferry](/wiki/Bridgeport_%26_Port_Jefferson_Ferry "Bridgeport & Port Jefferson Ferry") travels between [Bridgeport, Connecticut](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"), and [Port Jefferson, New York](/wiki/Port_Jefferson,_New_York "Port Jefferson, New York").[[215]](#cite_note-:1-218) Ferry service also operates out of [New London](/wiki/New_London,_Connecticut "New London, Connecticut") to [Orient, New York](/wiki/Orient,_New_York "Orient, New York"); [Fishers Island, New York](/wiki/Fishers_Island,_New_York "Fishers Island, New York"); and [Block Island, Rhode Island](/wiki/Block_Island,_Rhode_Island "Block Island, Rhode Island"), which are popular tourist destinations.[[215]](#cite_note-:1-218) Two ferries cross the Connecticut River: the [Rocky Hill–Glastonbury ferry](/wiki/Rocky_Hill%E2%80%93Glastonbury_ferry "Rocky Hill–Glastonbury ferry") and the [Chester–Hadlyme ferry](/wiki/Chester%E2%80%93Hadlyme_Ferry "Chester–Hadlyme Ferry"), the former of which is the oldest continuously operating ferry in the United States, operating since 1655.[[215]](#cite_note-:1-218)

[Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") has been the sole capital of Connecticut since 1875. Before then, [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") and Hartford alternated as dual capitals.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1)

### Constitutional history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Constitutional history")]

Main article: [History of the Connecticut Constitution](/wiki/History_of_the_Connecticut_Constitution "History of the Connecticut Constitution")

Connecticut is known as the "Constitution State". The origin of this nickname is uncertain, but it likely comes from Connecticut's pivotal role in the federal constitutional convention of 1787, during which [Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman") and [Oliver Ellsworth](/wiki/Oliver_Ellsworth "Oliver Ellsworth") helped to orchestrate what became known as the [Connecticut Compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise"), or the Great Compromise. This plan combined the [Virginia Plan](/wiki/Virginia_Plan "Virginia Plan") and the [New Jersey Plan](/wiki/New_Jersey_Plan "New Jersey Plan") to form a bicameral legislature, a form copied by almost every state constitution since the adoption of the federal constitution. Variations of the bicameral legislature had been proposed by Virginia and New Jersey, but Connecticut's plan was the one that was in effect until the early 20th century, when Senators ceased to be selected by their state legislatures and were instead directly elected. Otherwise, it is still the design of Congress.

The nickname also might refer to the [Fundamental Orders](/wiki/Fundamental_Orders_of_Connecticut "Fundamental Orders of Connecticut") of 1638–39. These Fundamental Orders represent the framework for the first formal Connecticut state government written by a representative body in Connecticut. The State of Connecticut government has operated under the direction of four separate documents in the course of the state's [constitutional history](/wiki/History_of_the_Connecticut_Constitution "History of the Connecticut Constitution"). After the Fundamental Orders, Connecticut was granted governmental authority by King [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") through the Connecticut Charter of 1662.

Separate branches of government did not exist during this period, and the General Assembly acted as the supreme authority. A constitution similar to the modern [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/U.S._Constitution "U.S. Constitution") was not adopted in Connecticut until 1818. Finally, the current state constitution was implemented in 1965. The 1965 constitution absorbed a majority of its 1818 predecessor, but incorporated a handful of important modifications.

### Executive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Executive")]

The governor heads the executive branch. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), [Ned Lamont](/wiki/Ned_Lamont "Ned Lamont") is the [Governor](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Connecticut "List of Governors of Connecticut")[[216]](#cite_note-219) and [Susan Bysiewicz](/wiki/Susan_Bysiewicz "Susan Bysiewicz") is the [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/List_of_Lieutenant_Governors_of_Connecticut "List of Lieutenant Governors of Connecticut");[[217]](#cite_note-220) both are Democrats. From 1639 until the adoption of the 1818 constitution, the governor presided over the General Assembly. In 1974, [Ella Grasso](/wiki/Ella_Grasso "Ella Grasso") was elected as the governor of Connecticut. This was the first time in United States history when a woman was a governor without her husband being governor first.[[98]](#cite_note-Pumont-99)

There are several executive departments: Administrative Services, Agriculture, Banking, Children and Families, Consumer Protection, Correction, Economic and Community Development, [Developmental Services](/wiki/Connecticut_Department_of_Developmental_Services "Connecticut Department of Developmental Services"), Construction Services, Education, Emergency Management and Public Protection, Energy & Environmental Protection, Higher Education, Insurance, Labor, Mental Health and Addiction Services, Military, Motor Vehicles, Public Health, Public Utility Regulatory Authority, Public Works, Revenue Services, Social Services, [Transportation](/wiki/Connecticut_Department_of_Transportation "Connecticut Department of Transportation"), and Veterans Affairs. In addition to these departments, there are other independent bureaus, offices and commissions.[[218]](#cite_note-221)

In addition to the governor and lieutenant governor, there are four other executive officers named in the state constitution that are elected directly by voters: secretary of the state, treasurer, comptroller, and attorney general. All executive officers are elected to four-year terms.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1)

### Legislative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Legislative")]

Connecticut's legislative branch is known as the [General Assembly](/wiki/Connecticut_General_Assembly "Connecticut General Assembly"). It is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") legislature consisting of an upper body, the [State Senate](/wiki/Connecticut_Senate "Connecticut Senate") (36 senators); and a lower body, the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Connecticut_House_of_Representatives "Connecticut House of Representatives") (151 representatives).[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1) Bills must pass each house in order to become law. The governor can veto bills, but this veto can be overridden by a two-thirds majority in both houses. Per Article XV of the state constitution, Senators and Representatives must be at least 18 years of age and are elected to two-year terms in November on even-numbered years. There also must always be between 30 and 50 senators and 125 to 225 representatives. The Lieutenant Governor presides over the Senate, except when absent from the chamber, when the [President pro tempore](/wiki/Presidents_pro_tempore_of_the_Connecticut_Senate "Presidents pro tempore of the Connecticut Senate") presides. The [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") presides over the House.[[219]](#cite_note-CT_CONST-222) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), [Matthew Ritter](/wiki/Matthew_Ritter "Matthew Ritter") is the Speaker of the House of Connecticut.

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), Connecticut's [United States Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senators "United States Senators") are [Richard Blumenthal](/wiki/Richard_Blumenthal "Richard Blumenthal") (Democrat) and [Chris Murphy](/wiki/Chris_Murphy "Chris Murphy") (Democrat).[[220]](#cite_note-223) Connecticut has five [representatives in the U.S. House](/wiki/U.S._Congressional_Delegations_from_Connecticut "U.S. Congressional Delegations from Connecticut"), all of whom are Democrats.[[221]](#cite_note-224)

Locally elected representatives also develop [local ordinances](/wiki/Local_ordinance "Local ordinance") to govern cities and towns.[[222]](#cite_note-225) The town ordinances often include [noise control](/wiki/Noise_pollution#Humans "Noise pollution") and [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning") guidelines.[[223]](#cite_note-226) However, the State of Connecticut also provides statewide ordinances for noise control as well.[[224]](#cite_note-227)

### Judicial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Judicial")]

The highest [court](/wiki/Court "Court") of Connecticut's judicial branch is the [Connecticut Supreme Court](/wiki/Connecticut_Supreme_Court "Connecticut Supreme Court"), headed by the Chief Justice of Connecticut. The Supreme Court is responsible for deciding on the constitutionality of laws, or cases as they relate to the law. Its proceedings are similar to those of the United States Supreme Court: no testimony is given by witnesses, and the lawyers of the two sides each present oral arguments no longer than thirty minutes. Following a court proceeding, the court may take several months to arrive at a judgment. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), the [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice "Chief Justice") is [Richard A. Robinson](/wiki/Richard_A._Robinson "Richard A. Robinson").

In 1818, the court became a separate entity, independent of the legislative and executive branches.[[225]](#cite_note-228) The [Connecticut Appellate Court](/wiki/Connecticut_Appellate_Court "Connecticut Appellate Court") is a lesser statewide court, and the Superior Courts are lower courts that resemble county courts of other states.

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Local government")]

Main article: [Local government in Connecticut](/wiki/Local_government_in_Connecticut "Local government in Connecticut")

See also: [List of counties in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Connecticut "List of counties in Connecticut"), [Councils of governments in Connecticut](/wiki/Councils_of_governments_in_Connecticut "Councils of governments in Connecticut"), [List of municipalities in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Connecticut "List of municipalities in Connecticut"), and [Borough (Connecticut)](/wiki/Borough_(Connecticut) "Borough (Connecticut)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Planning_Regions_of_Connecticut.png/250px-Planning_Regions_of_Connecticut.png)](/wiki/File:Planning_Regions_of_Connecticut.png)

Planning Regions of Connecticut

Connecticut does not have [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") government, unlike all other states except [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"). Connecticut county governments were mostly eliminated in 1960, with the exception of [sheriffs](/wiki/Sheriffs_in_the_United_States "Sheriffs in the United States") elected in each county.[[226]](#cite_note-229) In 2000, the county sheriff was abolished and replaced with the [state marshal](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Marshal "Connecticut State Marshal") system, which has districts that follow the old county territories. The judicial system is divided into judicial districts at the trial-court level which largely follow the old county lines.[[227]](#cite_note-230) The [eight counties](/wiki/List_of_Connecticut_counties "List of Connecticut counties") are still widely used for purely geographical and statistical purposes, such as [weather reports](/wiki/Weather_forecasting "Weather forecasting") and census reporting, although the latter ceased to use the counties in 2024.

The state is divided into nine regional [councils of government](/wiki/Councils_of_governments_in_Connecticut "Councils of governments in Connecticut") defined by the state Office of Planning and Management, which facilitate regional planning and coordination of services between member towns.[[228]](#cite_note-OPM-2-231) The Intragovernmental Policy Division of this Office coordinates regional planning with the administrative bodies of these regions. Each region has an administrative body made up chief executive officers of the member towns. The regions are established for the purpose of planning "coordination of regional and state planning activities; redesignation of logical planning regions and promotion of the continuation of regional planning organizations within the state; and provision for technical aid and the administration of financial assistance to regional planning organizations".[[228]](#cite_note-OPM-2-231) By 2015, the State of Connecticut recognized COGs as [county equivalents](/wiki/County_equivalent "County equivalent"), allowing them to apply for funding and grants made available to county governments in other states. In 2019 the state recommended to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") that the nine Councils of Governments replace its counties for statistical purposes.[[229]](#cite_note-232) This proposal was approved by the Census Bureau in 2022 and was fully implemented by 2024.[[230]](#cite_note-233)

Connecticut shares with the rest of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") a governmental institution called the [New England town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town"). The state is divided into 169 towns which serve as the fundamental political jurisdictions.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1) There are also 21 cities,[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1) most of which simply follow the boundaries of their namesake towns and have a merged city-town government. There are two exceptions: the [City of Groton](/wiki/Groton_(city),_Connecticut "Groton (city), Connecticut"), which is a subsection of the [Town of Groton](/wiki/Groton_(town),_Connecticut "Groton (town), Connecticut"), and the City of [Winsted](/wiki/Winsted,_Connecticut "Winsted, Connecticut") in the Town of [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester,_Connecticut "Winchester, Connecticut"). There are also nine incorporated [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(Connecticut) "Borough (Connecticut)") which may provide additional services to a section of town.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1)[[231]](#cite_note-234) [Naugatuck](/wiki/Naugatuck "Naugatuck") is a consolidated town and borough.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Politics")]

Further information: [Political party strength in Connecticut](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Connecticut "Political party strength in Connecticut") and [Elections in Connecticut](/wiki/Elections_in_Connecticut "Elections in Connecticut")

For many decades, Connecticut has consistently been a [blue state](/wiki/Blue_state "Blue state"). As of 2024, both of its U.S. Senators, all five of its U.S. House representatives, as well as its Governor, Lt. Governor, Attorney General, and Secretary of State, are members of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"). The last Republican presidential candidate to win Connecticut's votes in the Electoral College was [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in 1988.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Connecticut_Political_Party_Registration_1958_-_2012.png/330px-Connecticut_Political_Party_Registration_1958_-_2012.png)](/wiki/File:Connecticut_Political_Party_Registration_1958_-_2012.png)

Connecticut political party registration 1958–2012, marked with presidential influence

### Registered voters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Registered voters")]

Connecticut residents who register to vote may declare an affiliation to a political party, may become unaffiliated at will, and may change affiliations subject to certain waiting periods. As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit), around 58% of registered voters are enrolled in a political party. The [Democratic Party of Connecticut](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_Connecticut "Democratic Party of Connecticut") is the largest party in the state by voter registration, with 36% of voters, followed by the [Connecticut Republican Party](/wiki/Connecticut_Republican_Party "Connecticut Republican Party") with approximately 21%. An additional 1.6% are registered to [third parties](/wiki/Third_party_(politics) "Third party (politics)"). As of 2022, 4 third parties have statewide enrollment privileges (meaning any state resident may register as a member), including the [Libertarian Party of Connecticut](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_of_Connecticut "Libertarian Party of Connecticut"), the [Independent Party of Connecticut](/wiki/Independent_Party_of_Connecticut "Independent Party of Connecticut"), the [Connecticut Green Party](/wiki/Connecticut_Green_Party "Connecticut Green Party"), and the [Connecticut Working Families Party](/wiki/Connecticut_Working_Families_Party "Connecticut Working Families Party").[[233]](#cite_note-236) Connecticut allows [electoral fusion](/wiki/Electoral_fusion "Electoral fusion"), where the same candidate can run on the ballot of more than one political party; this is often used by the Connecticut Working Families Party to cross-endorse Democratic candidates.[[234]](#cite_note-237)

### Voting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Voting")]

In July 2009, the Connecticut legislature overrode a veto by Governor [M. Jodi Rell](/wiki/M._Jodi_Rell "M. Jodi Rell") to pass [SustiNet](/wiki/SustiNet "SustiNet"), the first significant public-option health care reform legislation in the nation.[[236]](#cite_note-239)

In April 2012, both houses of the Connecticut state legislature passed a bill (20 to 16 and 86 to 62) that abolished [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States") for all future crimes, while 11 inmates who were waiting on the [death row](/wiki/Death_row "Death row") at the time could still be executed.[[237]](#cite_note-240)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Connecticut](/wiki/Education_in_Connecticut "Education in Connecticut")

Further information: [List of school districts in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Connecticut "List of school districts in Connecticut")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Yale_MMI.JPG/250px-Yale_MMI.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yale_MMI.JPG)

[Yale's](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") motto means "light and truth".

Connecticut ranked third in the nation for [educational performance](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_States "Education in the United States"), according to [Education Week](/wiki/Education_Week "Education Week")'s Quality Counts 2018 report. It earned an overall score of 83.5 out of 100 points. On average, the country received a score of 75.2.[[238]](#cite_note-Edweek-241) Connecticut posted a B-plus in the Chance-for-Success category, ranking fourth on factors that contribute to a person's success both within and outside the K-12 education system. Connecticut received a mark of B-plus and finished fourth for School Finance. It ranked 12th with a grade of C on the K-12 Achievement Index.[[238]](#cite_note-Edweek-241)

### K–12

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: K–12")]

See also: [Connecticut State Board of Education](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Board_of_Education "Connecticut State Board of Education")

#### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Public schools")]

[Hartford Public High School](/wiki/Hartford_Public_High_School "Hartford Public High School") (1638) is the third-oldest secondary school in the nation after the [Collegiate School](/wiki/Collegiate_School_(New_York) "Collegiate School (New York)") (1628) in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and the [Boston Latin School](/wiki/Boston_Latin_School "Boston Latin School") (1635). Today, the [Connecticut State Board of Education](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Board_of_Education "Connecticut State Board of Education") manages the public school system for children in grades K–12. Board of Education members are [appointed](/wiki/Cronyism "Cronyism") by the [Governor of Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Connecticut "List of Governors of Connecticut").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/UConn_Main.JPG/250px-UConn_Main.JPG)](/wiki/File:UConn_Main.JPG)

[University of Connecticut](/wiki/University_of_Connecticut "University of Connecticut"), the state's main public university

#### Private schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Private schools")]

Connecticut has a number of [private schools](/wiki/Independent_school "Independent school"). Private schools may file for approval by the state Department of Education, but are not required to. Per state law, private schools must file yearly attendance reports with the state.[[239]](#cite_note-242)

Notable private schools include [Choate Rosemary Hall](/wiki/Choate_Rosemary_Hall "Choate Rosemary Hall"), [The Hotchkiss School](/wiki/Hotchkiss_School "Hotchkiss School"), [Loomis Chaffee School](/wiki/Loomis_Chaffee_School "Loomis Chaffee School"), and [Taft School](/wiki/Taft_School "Taft School").

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

See also: [List of colleges and universities in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Connecticut "List of colleges and universities in Connecticut")

Connecticut was home to the nation's first law school, [Litchfield Law School](/wiki/Litchfield_Law_School "Litchfield Law School"), which operated from 1773 to 1833 in [Litchfield](/wiki/Litchfield,_Connecticut "Litchfield, Connecticut"). Well known universities in the state include [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [Wesleyan University](/wiki/Wesleyan_University "Wesleyan University"), [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College_(Connecticut) "Trinity College (Connecticut)"), [Sacred Heart University](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_University "Sacred Heart University"), [Fairfield University](/wiki/Fairfield_University "Fairfield University"), [Quinnipiac University](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University"), and the [University of Connecticut](/wiki/University_of_Connecticut "University of Connecticut"). The [Connecticut State University System](/wiki/Connecticut_State_University_System "Connecticut State University System") includes 4 state universities, and the state also has 12 community colleges. The [United States Coast Guard Academy](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Academy "United States Coast Guard Academy") is located in New London.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Sports")]

There are two Connecticut teams in the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"). The [Bridgeport Islanders](/wiki/Bridgeport_Islanders "Bridgeport Islanders") is a farm team for the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") which competes at the [Total Mortgage Arena](/wiki/Total_Mortgage_Arena "Total Mortgage Arena") in [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"). The [Hartford Wolf Pack](/wiki/Hartford_Wolf_Pack "Hartford Wolf Pack") is an affiliate of the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"); they play in the [PeoplesBank Arena](/wiki/PeoplesBank_Arena "PeoplesBank Arena") in Hartford.

The [Hartford Yard Goats](/wiki/Hartford_Yard_Goats "Hartford Yard Goats") of the [Eastern League](/wiki/Eastern_League_(1938%E2%80%93present) "Eastern League (1938–present)") are a AA affiliate of the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies"). Also, the [Norwich Sea Unicorns](/wiki/Norwich_Sea_Unicorns "Norwich Sea Unicorns") play in the [Futures Collegiate Baseball League](/wiki/Futures_Collegiate_Baseball_League "Futures Collegiate Baseball League"). The [New Britain Bees](/wiki/New_Britain_Bees "New Britain Bees") play in the [Atlantic League of Professional Baseball](/wiki/Atlantic_League_of_Professional_Baseball "Atlantic League of Professional Baseball"). The [Connecticut Sun](/wiki/Connecticut_Sun "Connecticut Sun") of the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") currently play at the [Mohegan Sun Arena](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun_Arena "Mohegan Sun Arena") in [Uncasville](/wiki/Uncasville,_Connecticut "Uncasville, Connecticut"). In soccer, [Hartford Athletic](/wiki/Hartford_Athletic "Hartford Athletic") began play in the [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship") in 2019.

The state hosts several major sporting events. Since 1952, a [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") golf tournament has been played in the Hartford area. It was originally called the "Insurance City Open" and later the "Greater Hartford Open" and is now known as the [Travelers Championship](/wiki/Travelers_Championship "Travelers Championship").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/LimeRock06_Aston_Porsche_Audi_at_Big_Bend.jpg/330px-LimeRock06_Aston_Porsche_Audi_at_Big_Bend.jpg)](/wiki/File:LimeRock06_Aston_Porsche_Audi_at_Big_Bend.jpg)

[Lime Rock](/wiki/Lime_Rock_Park "Lime Rock Park"), a home of the [American Le Mans Series](/wiki/American_Le_Mans_Series "American Le Mans Series")

[Lime Rock Park](/wiki/Lime_Rock_Park "Lime Rock Park") in Salisbury is a 1.5-mile (2.4 km) road racing course, home to the [International Motor Sports Association](/wiki/International_Motor_Sports_Association "International Motor Sports Association"), [SCCA](/wiki/SCCA "SCCA"), [United States Auto Club](/wiki/United_States_Auto_Club "United States Auto Club"), and [K&N Pro Series East](/wiki/K%26N_Pro_Series_East "K&N Pro Series East") races. [Thompson International Speedway](/wiki/Thompson_International_Speedway "Thompson International Speedway"), [Stafford Motor Speedway](/wiki/Stafford_Motor_Speedway "Stafford Motor Speedway"), and [Waterford Speedbowl](/wiki/Waterford_Speedbowl "Waterford Speedbowl") are oval tracks holding weekly races for NASCAR Modifieds and other classes, including the NASCAR Whelen Modified Tour. The state also hosts several major [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") events for [Bellator MMA](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA") and the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship").

### Professional sports teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Professional sports teams")]

The [Hartford Whalers](/wiki/Hartford_Whalers "Hartford Whalers") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") played in Hartford from 1975 to 1997 at the [Hartford Civic Center](/wiki/Hartford_Civic_Center "Hartford Civic Center"). They departed to [Raleigh, North Carolina](/wiki/Raleigh,_North_Carolina "Raleigh, North Carolina"), after disputes with the state over the construction of a new arena, and they are now known as the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes"). A baseball team known as the Hartfords (or [Hartford Dark Blues](/wiki/Hartford_Dark_Blues "Hartford Dark Blues")) played in the [National Association](/wiki/National_Association_of_Professional_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Professional Base Ball Players") from 1874 to 1875, before becoming charter members of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") in 1876. The team moved to [Brooklyn, New York](/wiki/Brooklyn,_New_York "Brooklyn, New York"), and then disbanded one season later. In 1926, Hartford also had a franchise in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") known as the [Hartford Blues](/wiki/Hartford_Blues "Hartford Blues").[[240]](#cite_note-HistoryNYG-243) From 2000 until 2006 the city was home to the [Hartford FoxForce](/wiki/Hartford_FoxForce "Hartford FoxForce") of [World TeamTennis](/wiki/World_TeamTennis "World TeamTennis").[[241]](#cite_note-244)

### College sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Connecticut&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: College sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg/330px-Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg)

Yale Bowl during "[The Game](/wiki/Harvard-Yale_football_games_(The_Game) "Harvard-Yale football games (The Game)")" between Yale and Harvard. The Bowl was also the home of the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL")'s [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") in 1973–74.

The [Connecticut Huskies](/wiki/Connecticut_Huskies "Connecticut Huskies") are the team of the University of Connecticut (UConn); they play [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") sports. Both the [men's basketball](/wiki/Connecticut_Huskies_men%27s_basketball "Connecticut Huskies men's basketball") and [women's basketball](/wiki/Connecticut_Huskies_women%27s_basketball "Connecticut Huskies women's basketball") teams have won multiple national championships. In 2004, UConn became the first school in NCAA Division I history to have its men's and women's basketball programs win the national title in the same year; they repeated the feat in 2014 and are still the only Division I school to win both titles in the same year.[[242]](#cite_note-245)[[243]](#cite_note-246) The UConn women's basketball team holds the record for the longest consecutive winning streak in NCAA college basketball at 111 games, a streak that ended in 2017.[[244]](#cite_note-247) Both teams play in the historic [Harry A. Gampel Pavilion](/wiki/Harry_A._Gampel_Pavilion "Harry A. Gampel Pavilion") and [PeoplesBank Arena](/wiki/PeoplesBank_Arena "PeoplesBank Arena") in Hartford. The [UConn Huskies football team](/wiki/UConn_Huskies_football_team "UConn Huskies football team") has played its home games at [Rentschler Field](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney_Stadium_at_Rentschler_Field "Pratt & Whitney Stadium at Rentschler Field") in East Hartford since 2003.[[245]](#cite_note-248)

New Haven biennially hosts "[The Game](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry")" between the [Yale Bulldogs](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs "Yale Bulldogs") and the [Harvard Crimson](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson "Harvard Crimson"), the country's second-oldest college football rivalry. Yale alumnus [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp") is deemed the "Father of American Football", and he helped develop modern football while living in New Haven.[[246]](#cite_note-249) Other Connecticut universities which feature Division I sports teams are [Quinnipiac University](/wiki/Quinnipiac_Bobcats "Quinnipiac Bobcats"), [Fairfield University](/wiki/Fairfield_Stags "Fairfield Stags"), [Central Connecticut State University](/wiki/Central_Connecticut_Blue_Devils "Central Connecticut Blue Devils") and [Sacred Heart University](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_Pioneers "Sacred Heart Pioneers").

The name "Connecticut" originated with the [Mohegan](/wiki/Mohegan "Mohegan") word *quonehtacut*, meaning "place of long tidal river".[[1]](#cite_note-AboutCT-1) Connecticut's official nickname is "The Constitution State", adopted in 1959 and based on its colonial constitution of 1638–1639 which was the first in America and, arguably, the world.[[4]](#cite_note-SOTS-4) Connecticut is also unofficially known as "The [Nutmeg](/wiki/Nutmeg "Nutmeg") State",[[4]](#cite_note-SOTS-4) whose origin is unknown. It may have come from its sailors returning from voyages with nutmeg, which was a very valuable spice in the 18th and 19th centuries. It may have originated in the early machined sheet tin nutmeg grinders sold by early Connecticut peddlers. It is also facetiously said to come from [Yankee](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee") peddlers from Connecticut who would sell small carved knobs of wood shaped to look like nutmeg to unsuspecting customers.[[247]](#cite_note-cslib-250) [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") gave Connecticut the title of "The Provisions State"[[4]](#cite_note-SOTS-4) because of the material aid that the state rendered to the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") effort. Connecticut is also known as "The Land of Steady Habits".[[4]](#cite_note-SOTS-4)

According to *Webster's New International Dictionary* (1993), a person who is a native or resident of Connecticut is a "Connecticuter". There are numerous other terms coined in print but not in use, such as "Connecticotian" ([Cotton Mather](/wiki/Cotton_Mather "Cotton Mather") in 1702) and "Connecticutensian" ([Samuel Peters](/wiki/Samuel_Peters_(clergyman) "Samuel Peters (clergyman)") in 1781). Linguist [Allen Walker Read](/wiki/Allen_Walker_Read "Allen Walker Read") suggests the more playful term "Connecticutie".[[248]](#cite_note-251) "[Nutmegger](/wiki/Nutmegger "Nutmegger")" is sometimes used,[[247]](#cite_note-cslib-250) as is "[Yankee](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee")".

The official state song is "[Yankee Doodle](/wiki/Yankee_Doodle "Yankee Doodle")". The traditional abbreviation of the state's name is "Conn."; the official [postal abbreviation](/wiki/United_States_postal_abbreviations "United States postal abbreviations") is CT.

[Commemorative stamps](/wiki/Commemorative_stamp "Commemorative stamp") issued by the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") with Connecticut themes include [Nathan Hale](/wiki/Nathan_Hale "Nathan Hale"), [Eugene O'Neill](/wiki/Eugene_O%27Neill "Eugene O'Neill"), [Josiah Willard Gibbs](/wiki/Josiah_Willard_Gibbs "Josiah Willard Gibbs"), [Noah Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster"), [Eli Whitney](/wiki/Eli_Whitney "Eli Whitney"), the [whaling](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling") ship the *[Charles W. Morgan](/wiki/Charles_W._Morgan_(ship) "Charles W. Morgan (ship)")*, which is docked at [Mystic Seaport](/wiki/Mystic_Seaport "Mystic Seaport"), and a [decoy](/wiki/Decoy "Decoy") of a broadbill [duck](/wiki/Duck "Duck").
